text: The port placement is as shown in the figure.
The red arrow depicts the position of the robot, the blue line the scapula, and the green the hilum.
The black lines represent the ports.
Once the ports are introduced, the pleura is examined.
The lung upper lobe is grasped close to the hilum with a forester clamp and the clamp itself can be left to hang by itself and its weight will retract the lung.
the nodule in the lingula of the left upper lobe is visualized.
The phrenic nerve is visualized, tented up.
Dissection is performed starting at the level 5 lymph nodes.
On cervical megastemoscopy, the frozen section revealed that the level 2, level 4, and level 7 lymph nodes were negative for cancer.
The level 5 lymph nodes are dissected out and also sent off for a frozen section.
Hemostasis is vital.
Care is taken to make sure that the recurrent laryngeal nerve is not injured in the iritoformulary window.
one can see two lymph nodes being dissected out.
These are handed off to the assistant who uses the axillary port, which will be the area where the lobe is going to be taken out in the future.
Dissection is then performed in the hilum.
We prefer to perform an anterior to posterior technique.
The first structure to be dissected out is the pulmonary veins.
It is very vital to define the anatomy, the venous anatomy.
Often there can be more than one lingular vein.
The lingular vein is just coming into view there.
The pleura is dissected all the way superiorly up to the pulmonary artery.
The main pulmonary artery, the left trunk, can be seen.
A 10L lymph node, which is usually found at the crotch between the vein and the artery, is dissected out and sent off for permanent pathology.
The assistant can also use the port in the back which is for the stapler.
Continued dissection of the pulmonary veins is performed.
The upper lobe vein is clearly visible in the superior aspect.
The inferior pulmonary vein is also defined to clarify the venous anatomy.
You can see there are two lingular branches.
Additional level 10L lymph nodes are dissected out.
These are the two branches of the lingular vein and a silk tie is used to encircle the lingular vein.
This helps in easy placement of the endo-GIA stapler with a vascular load to help transect the lingular vein.
The main upper lobe pulmonary vein is seen towards the left of the screen.
The vascular load for the endogia is then brought in at the posterior port, which is just above the diaphragm, and it is a 12mm port.
The lingular vein is transected.
The silk suture can be left in the field as this will be used in the future to encircle the lingular bronchus and the artery at a later stage.
Continued dissection with resection of the 11L lymph nodes can be seen.
The advantage with the robot is extensive lymph node dissection can be performed with good hemostasis.
As in this case, over 30 lymph nodes were resected in the final pathology specimen.
as the lymph node dissection is performed hemostasis is achieved at the same time next we will move on to defining the bronchial anatomy it is very essential to encircle the
lingular bronchus and to confirm that the remainder of the upper lobe bronchus and the lower lobe bronchus are widely patent prior to transection of the lingular bronchus.
The lower lobe bronchus is coming into view right by the sucker.
The lymph nodes in this area over the bronchi are being resected to help
dealing with anatomy and also achieving a better lymph node yield.
One can clearly see the bronchial anatomy coming into view.
The lower lobe bronchus with the bronchial artery was seen just below the sucker.
the crotch of the lingular bronchus and the upper lobe bronchus is being dissected there and the dissection has been performed behind the lingular bronchus to completely encircle it.
The silk suture used to
retract the pulmonary vein previously is kept in the field and now it can be used to retract the lingual bronchus.
EndoGIA stapler is then brought into the field.
Care is taken not to injure the pulmonary vein or the pulmonary artery while placing the stapler.
prior to closing the stapler the silk suture is removed from the field and the stapler is occluded but not fired.
At this time anesthesia is asked to ventilate the patient to make sure that the remainder of the upper lobe
and the lower lobe are adequately ventilated.
One can clearly see that the remainder of the lung is being ventilated.
The lobar bronchus is then transected.

the staple line of the specimen side can be used as a handle.
There's often lymph nodes in this area.
One can see that the hemostasis in this patient is a little more difficult than usual as the patient was on Coumadin preoperatively for atrial fibrillation.
The lingular artery is being dissected out.
There is additional 11 lymph nodes which are being dissected.
The inferior pulmonary ligament is then released and level 9 lymph nodes
in the inferior pulmonary ligament are also dissected out and sent off to permanent pathology.
The inferior pulmonary ligament is released until the inferior pulmonary vein is visualized.
The major fissure is incomplete in this patient, so endogia stapler is then used to complete the fissure to help separate the lingula from the lower lobe.
If the patient is extensively emphysematous, we do use vicryl reinforced staple loads, as in this case here.
The fissure is completed all the way up to the hilum.
This one helps with the completion of the fissure but two also helps access to the lingular artery and also allows the stapler to come in at a much more easier angle.
The lingular artery has been encircled there.

which has been left intercorporeally is used to encircle the pulmonary artery, the lingular branch.
Retraction is performed.
The endogia stapler with the vascular load is then brought into the field.

the remainder of the upper lobe pulmonary artery the
pulmonary artery, the lingular branch is divided.
In this patient, just a single lingular artery branch was seen.
No additional pulmonary artery branches were found.
If an additional clip is required to the pulmonary artery branch, that can be placed at this point of time.
The fissure is completed.
Next, the lingula is separated from the remainder of the upper lobe, reinforced staple line can be seen.
Often the last staple load requires scissors to cut the micro strip at the tip of the
staple line.
The harmonic is changed out for a hook cautery and the axillary port site which is usually placed in the second intercostal space just above the third rib is scored with electrocautery.
This is usually the widest intercostal space and allows for easy removal of the specimen.
Endocatch bag is used.
You can see that we routinely mark the inside of the endocatch bag as sometimes identification of the inside and outside becomes difficult.
The specimen and any mediastinal lymph nodes which are dissected out previously are placed in the specimen bag.
The specimen bag is withdrawn all the way to the chest wall but not removed.
This allows the CO2 to continue to stay and help visualize the lung better.
We irrigate the chest cavity and a leak test is performed.
No bronchial stump leak is seen.
A marking multilevel intercostal nerve block is performed.
